answer:
Tool Calling — Debug Scenario

Core Definition

Tool calling (function calling) allows an LLM to request the execution of external functions during a conversation. The model outputs structured JSON specifying which function to call and with what arguments, the application executes the function, and the result is fed back to the model for generating a final response.

Manoj's Project Context

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj integrated an AI assistant that could check leave balances and submit leave requests via tool calling. He debugged an issue where the model hallucinated function arguments — passing invalid date formats that crashed the leave API.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

import Anthropic from '@anthropic-ai/sdk';
import { z } from 'zod';

const client = new Anthropic();

// Define tools with strict schemas
const tools = [
  {
    name: 'check_leave_balance',
    description: 'Check remaining leave balance for an employee',
    input_schema: {
      type: 'object',
      properties: {
        employee_id: { type: 'string', description: 'Employee ID (e.g., EMP-001)' },
        leave_type: { type: 'string', enum: ['annual', 'sick', 'personal'] },
      },
      required: ['employee_id', 'leave_type'],
    },
  },
  {
    name: 'submit_leave_request',
    description: 'Submit a new leave request',
    input_schema: {
      type: 'object',
      properties: {
        employee_id: { type: 'string' },
        start_date: { type: 'string', description: 'ISO 8601 date: YYYY-MM-DD' },
        end_date: { type: 'string', description: 'ISO 8601 date: YYYY-MM-DD' },
        leave_type: { type: 'string', enum: ['annual', 'sick', 'personal'] },
        reason: { type: 'string' },
      },
      required: ['employee_id', 'start_date', 'end_date', 'leave_type'],
    },
  },
];

// Validation schemas (the fix for hallucinated args)
const LeaveRequestSchema = z.object({
  employee_id: z.string().regex(/^EMP-\d{3,}$/),
  start_date: z.string().regex(/^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$/),
  end_date: z.string().regex(/^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$/),
  leave_type: z.enum(['annual', 'sick', 'personal']),
  reason: z.string().optional(),
});

async function handleToolCall(name: string, input: unknown) {
  // ALWAYS validate before executing — models can hallucinate args
  if (name === 'submit_leave_request') {
    const parsed = LeaveRequestSchema.safeParse(input);
    if (!parsed.success) {
      return { error: `Invalid arguments: ${parsed.error.message}` };
    }
    return await leaveApi.submit(parsed.data);
  }
}

// Conversation loop with tool use
async function chat(userMessage: string) {
  const response = await client.messages.create({
    model: 'claude-sonnet-4-6',
    max_tokens: 1024,
    tools,
    messages: [{ role: 'user', content: userMessage }],
  });

  if (response.stop_reason === 'tool_use') {
    const toolBlock = response.content.find(b => b.type === 'tool_use');
    const result = await handleToolCall(toolBlock.name, toolBlock.input);
    // Feed result back to model for final response
    // ...
  }
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

The HR AI assistant crashed when a user asked "book me a leave next Friday." The model called `submit_leave_request` with `start_date: "next Friday"` instead of an ISO date. Manoj's fix: add Zod validation between the model's tool call output and the actual function execution. The validator rejects malformed arguments and returns a descriptive error message to the model, which then self-corrects on the next turn. He also improved the tool schema descriptions to explicitly state "ISO 8601 format: YYYY-MM-DD" and added examples in the description.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Model hallucinates function argumentsAlways validate with Zod/JSON Schema before executing
Tool descriptions too vagueInclude format examples, enums, and constraints in descriptions
Missing error feedback loopReturn validation errors to the model so it can self-correct

How to Validate

  • Test with ambiguous date inputs — verify validation catches them
  • Confirm model self-corrects after receiving validation error
  • Check that all tool schemas have explicit format descriptions
  • Verify no raw user input reaches the API without validation

Tool Calling — Compare Two Approaches

Core Definition

Two approaches to LLM tool calling architecture: single-turn tool execution (model calls one tool, gets result, responds) versus agentic multi-turn loops (model can call multiple tools in sequence, reasoning between calls to complete complex tasks).

Manoj's Project Context

For the HR Leave Management System, Manoj compared single-turn tool use (answer one question at a time) versus an agentic loop where the AI assistant could chain multiple actions — check balance, calculate overlap with team calendar, and submit the request — in one conversation turn.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// Approach A — Single-turn tool execution
async function singleTurnChat(userMsg: string) {
  const response = await client.messages.create({
    model: 'claude-sonnet-4-6',
    max_tokens: 1024,
    tools,
    messages: [{ role: 'user', content: userMsg }],
  });

  if (response.stop_reason === 'tool_use') {
    const toolCall = response.content.find(b => b.type === 'tool_use')!;
    const result = await executeTool(toolCall.name, toolCall.input);

    // One tool call, then final response
    const final = await client.messages.create({
      model: 'claude-sonnet-4-6',
      max_tokens: 1024,
      tools,
      messages: [
        { role: 'user', content: userMsg },
        { role: 'assistant', content: response.content },
        { role: 'user', content: [{ type: 'tool_result', tool_use_id: toolCall.id, content: JSON.stringify(result) }] },
      ],
    });
    return extractText(final);
  }
  return extractText(response);
}

// Approach B — Agentic loop (HR Leave System choice for complex flows)
async function agenticChat(userMsg: string, maxIterations = 5) {
  const messages = [{ role: 'user', content: userMsg }];

  for (let i = 0; i < maxIterations; i++) {
    const response = await client.messages.create({
      model: 'claude-sonnet-4-6',
      max_tokens: 1024,
      tools,
      messages,
    });

    messages.push({ role: 'assistant', content: response.content });

    if (response.stop_reason === 'end_turn') {
      return extractText(response); // Done — no more tool calls
    }

    // Execute all tool calls in this turn
    const toolResults = [];
    for (const block of response.content) {
      if (block.type === 'tool_use') {
        const result = await executeTool(block.name, block.input);
        toolResults.push({
          type: 'tool_result',
          tool_use_id: block.id,
          content: JSON.stringify(result),
        });
      }
    }
    messages.push({ role: 'user', content: toolResults });
  }

  return 'Max iterations reached — please try a simpler request.';
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

Initially the HR assistant used single-turn mode. When a user asked "Book me annual leave next week if I have enough balance," it could only check the balance but couldn't proceed to book. Manoj upgraded to an agentic loop: the model first calls `check_leave_balance`, sees the result (12 days remaining), then calls `submit_leave_request`. He added a `maxIterations` safeguard to prevent infinite loops and logged each tool call for auditability. For simple queries like "What's my balance?", the model naturally exits after one tool call.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Agentic loop runs too many iterationsSet maxIterations limit (5 is a good default)
Higher token costs with multi-turnUse single-turn for simple queries; agentic only for workflows
Hard to debug multi-step reasoningLog each tool call and result; provide trace IDs

How to Validate

  • Test complex workflow: "Book leave if I have balance" → verify 2 tool calls
  • Test simple query: "What's my balance?" → verify 1 tool call only
  • Verify maxIterations prevents infinite loops
  • Check all tool calls are logged with timestamps

Context Window — Design Implementation

Core Definition

The context window is the maximum number of tokens an LLM can process in a single request (input + output). Managing context window efficiently means strategically selecting what information to include, using techniques like summarization, sliding windows, and RAG to work within token limits while maintaining conversation quality.

Manoj's Project Context

In the HR Leave Management System, the AI assistant needed to handle long conversation histories about leave policies. Manoj designed a context management strategy that summarized older messages while keeping recent exchanges and relevant policy documents within the token budget.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

import Anthropic from '@anthropic-ai/sdk';

const client = new Anthropic();
const MAX_CONTEXT_TOKENS = 8000; // Budget for input
const SYSTEM_PROMPT_TOKENS = 500;
const OUTPUT_TOKENS = 1024;

interface Message {
  role: 'user' | 'assistant';
  content: string;
  tokenCount: number;
}

class ContextManager {
  private messages: Message[] = [];
  private summary: string = '';

  async addMessage(msg: Message) {
    this.messages.push(msg);
    await this.trimIfNeeded();
  }

  private async trimIfNeeded() {
    const totalTokens = this.messages.reduce((sum, m) => sum + m.tokenCount, 0);
    const budget = MAX_CONTEXT_TOKENS - SYSTEM_PROMPT_TOKENS;

    if (totalTokens > budget) {
      // Summarize oldest messages
      const toSummarize = this.messages.splice(0, Math.ceil(this.messages.length / 2));
      const summaryText = toSummarize.map(m => `${m.role}: ${m.content}`).join('\n');

      const summaryResponse = await client.messages.create({
        model: 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001', // Use fast model for summarization
        max_tokens: 200,
        messages: [{
          role: 'user',
          content: `Summarize this conversation concisely, preserving key facts and decisions:\n${summaryText}`,
        }],
      });

      this.summary = summaryResponse.content[0].text;
    }
  }

  buildMessages(): Anthropic.MessageParam[] {
    const msgs: Anthropic.MessageParam[] = [];
    if (this.summary) {
      msgs.push({ role: 'user', content: `[Previous conversation summary: ${this.summary}]` });
      msgs.push({ role: 'assistant', content: 'Understood, I have the context from our previous conversation.' });
    }
    msgs.push(...this.messages.map(m => ({ role: m.role, content: m.content })));
    return msgs;
  }
}

// Usage in HR Leave Assistant
const context = new ContextManager();

async function chat(userMessage: string) {
  await context.addMessage({ role: 'user', content: userMessage, tokenCount: estimateTokens(userMessage) });

  const response = await client.messages.create({
    model: 'claude-sonnet-4-6',
    max_tokens: OUTPUT_TOKENS,
    system: HR_SYSTEM_PROMPT,
    messages: context.buildMessages(),
  });

  const text = response.content[0].text;
  await context.addMessage({ role: 'assistant', content: text, tokenCount: response.usage.output_tokens });
  return text;
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

The HR assistant started giving irrelevant answers after long conversations about leave policies. Manoj discovered the conversation history exceeded the context window, causing the API to truncate early messages containing important policy context. He implemented a ContextManager that uses Claude Haiku to summarize older messages when the total exceeds 80% of the budget. He reserved token budget for the system prompt (500), summary (300), recent messages (2000), and RAG context (1000), ensuring the model always has access to relevant policy information even in long conversations.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Context silently truncated, losing important infoTrack token usage; proactively summarize before hitting limits
Summarization loses critical detailsKeep key facts (names, dates, decisions) in structured format
No budget reserved for RAG contextPre-allocate token budgets: system prompt, history, RAG, output

How to Validate

  • Simulate a 20-turn conversation and verify context stays within budget
  • Check that summarized context preserves key facts from early messages
  • Verify RAG context is included even in long conversations
  • Test that response quality doesn't degrade after summarization

Context Window — Debug Scenario

Core Definition

Context window debugging involves diagnosing issues caused by exceeding token limits, losing important context through truncation, or inefficiently using the available context space. Common symptoms include degraded response quality, missing information in answers, and API errors for exceeding max tokens.

Manoj's Project Context

In ContactsHub, Manoj debugged an issue where the AI-powered contact search assistant "forgot" search filters mentioned earlier in the conversation. The root cause was that the conversation history was being sent in full, eventually exceeding the context window and causing silent truncation of early messages.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// Problem: Naive approach sends full history
const messages = conversationHistory; // Grows unbounded!
const response = await client.messages.create({
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-6',
  max_tokens: 1024,
  messages, // Eventually exceeds context window
});

// Fix: Extract structured state + sliding window
interface SearchState {
  activeFilters: { field: string; value: string }[];
  sortBy: string;
  lastQuery: string;
}

class ContactAssistant {
  private state: SearchState = { activeFilters: [], sortBy: 'name', lastQuery: '' };
  private recentMessages: Anthropic.MessageParam[] = [];
  private readonly MAX_MESSAGES = 10;

  async chat(userMessage: string) {
    // Update state from user message (structured extraction)
    await this.updateStateFromMessage(userMessage);

    // Keep only recent messages (sliding window)
    this.recentMessages.push({ role: 'user', content: userMessage });
    if (this.recentMessages.length > this.MAX_MESSAGES) {
      this.recentMessages = this.recentMessages.slice(-this.MAX_MESSAGES);
    }

    // Include current state in system prompt — never lost!
    const systemPrompt = `You are a contact search assistant.
Current search state:
- Active filters: ${JSON.stringify(this.state.activeFilters)}
- Sort: ${this.state.sortBy}
- Last query: ${this.state.lastQuery}
Use this state to maintain context across the conversation.`;

    const response = await client.messages.create({
      model: 'claude-sonnet-4-6',
      max_tokens: 1024,
      system: systemPrompt,
      messages: this.recentMessages,
    });

    const text = response.content[0].text;
    this.recentMessages.push({ role: 'assistant', content: text });
    return text;
  }

  private async updateStateFromMessage(msg: string) {
    // Use a cheap model to extract structured state updates
    const extraction = await client.messages.create({
      model: 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001',
      max_tokens: 200,
      messages: [{
        role: 'user',
        content: `Extract any search filters from: "${msg}"
Return JSON: { filters: [{field, value}], sortBy?: string }`,
      }],
    });
    // Merge extracted filters into state...
  }
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, after about 10 messages, the AI assistant would stop applying previously mentioned filters like "show only contacts from Mumbai." Manoj logged the token counts and found that by message 12, the total exceeded 8K tokens, and the API was silently dropping early messages. His fix: extract structured search state (active filters, sort order) into a `SearchState` object that gets injected into the system prompt on every request. The sliding window keeps only the last 10 messages for conversational context, while the structured state ensures filters are never lost regardless of conversation length.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Silent truncation of early messagesTrack token counts; use sliding window or summarization
Important state lost in truncated messagesExtract key state into structured objects; include in system prompt
Token estimation inaccurateUse the API's usage.input_tokens from responses to calibrate

How to Validate

  • Send 20+ messages with filters mentioned early — verify filters persist
  • Log token counts per request — confirm they stay within budget
  • Verify structured state correctly extracted from natural language
  • Test that sliding window preserves conversational coherence

Retrieval Quality — Design Implementation

Core Definition

Retrieval quality in RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) systems measures how well the retrieval component finds relevant documents to ground the LLM's responses. High retrieval quality means the top-K retrieved chunks contain the information needed to answer the user's question accurately.

Manoj's Project Context

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj built a RAG pipeline for the leave policy chatbot. He designed retrieval quality improvements after the bot gave wrong answers about carry-over policies because the retriever returned irrelevant chunks from the employee handbook.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// retrieval-pipeline.ts — Multi-stage retrieval with quality optimization
import { OpenAIEmbeddings } from 'langchain/embeddings/openai';

interface Chunk {
  id: string;
  text: string;
  metadata: { source: string; section: string; page: number };
  score?: number;
}

class PolicyRetriever {
  private embeddings: OpenAIEmbeddings;

  constructor() {
    this.embeddings = new OpenAIEmbeddings({ modelName: 'text-embedding-3-small' });
  }

  async retrieve(query: string, topK = 5): Promise<Chunk[]> {
    // Stage 1: Query expansion — generate alternative phrasings
    const expandedQueries = await this.expandQuery(query);

    // Stage 2: Vector search with expanded queries
    const allResults: Chunk[] = [];
    for (const q of expandedQueries) {
      const embedding = await this.embeddings.embedQuery(q);
      const results = await vectorStore.similaritySearch(embedding, topK * 2);
      allResults.push(...results);
    }

    // Stage 3: Deduplicate
    const unique = this.deduplicateByContent(allResults);

    // Stage 4: Re-rank using cross-encoder or LLM-based scoring
    const reranked = await this.rerank(query, unique);

    return reranked.slice(0, topK);
  }

  private async expandQuery(query: string): Promise<string[]> {
    const response = await client.messages.create({
      model: 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001',
      max_tokens: 200,
      messages: [{
        role: 'user',
        content: `Generate 3 alternative phrasings for this HR policy question:
"${query}"
Return as JSON array of strings.`,
      }],
    });
    const alternatives = JSON.parse(response.content[0].text);
    return [query, ...alternatives];
  }

  private async rerank(query: string, chunks: Chunk[]): Promise<Chunk[]> {
    // LLM-based relevance scoring
    const scored = await Promise.all(
      chunks.map(async (chunk) => {
        const response = await client.messages.create({
          model: 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001',
          max_tokens: 10,
          messages: [{
            role: 'user',
            content: `Rate relevance 0-10 of this text to the query.
Query: ${query}
Text: ${chunk.text.slice(0, 500)}
Return only the number.`,
          }],
        });
        chunk.score = parseInt(response.content[0].text) || 0;
        return chunk;
      })
    );
    return scored.sort((a, b) => (b.score ?? 0) - (a.score ?? 0));
  }
}

// Chunking strategy — critical for retrieval quality
function chunkPolicyDocument(doc: string): Chunk[] {
  // Chunk by section headers, not fixed character count
  const sections = doc.split(/^## /gm);
  return sections.map((section, i) => ({
    id: `policy-${i}`,
    text: section.trim(),
    metadata: {
      source: 'leave-policy-2024.md',
      section: section.split('\n')[0],
      page: i,
    },
  }));
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

The HR chatbot answered "Can I carry over unused leave?" with generic information instead of the specific carry-over policy (max 5 days, must be used by March). Manoj traced the issue: the retriever returned chunks about "leave types" instead of "carry-over rules" because the embedding similarity was higher for the more general chunks. He fixed it by: (1) chunking the policy document by section headers instead of fixed character counts, (2) adding query expansion to generate alternative phrasings like "leave rollover policy," and (3) implementing LLM-based re-ranking to score chunk relevance. After these improvements, the correct carry-over policy chunk ranked #1.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Fixed-size chunks split important contextChunk by semantic boundaries (sections, paragraphs)
Single query misses relevant docsQuery expansion generates alternative phrasings
Vector search returns superficially similar but irrelevant chunksAdd re-ranking stage with cross-encoder or LLM scoring

How to Validate

  • Create test set of 20 policy questions with expected chunk IDs
  • Measure Precision@5 and Recall@5 before and after improvements
  • Verify carry-over policy question returns correct chunk as #1 result
  • Compare retrieval quality with and without query expansion

Retrieval Quality — Debug Scenario

Core Definition

Retrieval quality debugging involves diagnosing why a RAG system retrieves irrelevant or incomplete documents, leading to poor LLM responses. Common issues include bad chunking strategies, embedding model limitations, missing metadata filters, and insufficient overlap between chunks.

Manoj's Project Context

In ContactsHub, Manoj added an AI feature to search contacts using natural language ("find my colleagues from the Mumbai office who work in engineering"). The retrieval quality was poor — it returned contacts from Mumbai but ignored the engineering department filter.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// BEFORE: Pure vector search (buggy)
async function searchContacts(query: string) {
  const embedding = await embedQuery(query);
  // Problem: vector similarity doesn't reliably filter by department
  const results = await db.collection('contacts').aggregate([
    {
      $vectorSearch: {
        queryVector: embedding,
        path: 'embedding',
        numCandidates: 100,
        limit: 10,
      },
    },
  ]).toArray();
  return results;
}

// AFTER: Hybrid search with metadata extraction + pre-filtering
async function searchContactsFixed(query: string) {
  // Step 1: Extract structured filters from natural language
  const filters = await extractFilters(query);
  // Returns: { city: 'Mumbai', department: 'engineering' }

  // Step 2: Build MongoDB filter from extracted metadata
  const metadataFilter: Record = {};
  if (filters.city) metadataFilter.city = { $regex: filters.city, $options: 'i' };
  if (filters.department) metadataFilter.department = { $regex: filters.department, $options: 'i' };

  // Step 3: Vector search with pre-filter
  const embedding = await embedQuery(query);
  const results = await db.collection('contacts').aggregate([
    {
      $vectorSearch: {
        queryVector: embedding,
        path: 'embedding',
        numCandidates: 100,
        limit: 10,
        filter: metadataFilter, // Pre-filter before vector ranking
      },
    },
  ]).toArray();
  return results;
}

async function extractFilters(query: string) {
  const response = await client.messages.create({
    model: 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001',
    max_tokens: 100,
    messages: [{
      role: 'user',
      content: `Extract structured filters from: "${query}"
Return JSON with optional fields: city, department, role, name.
Only include fields explicitly mentioned.`,
    }],
  });
  return JSON.parse(response.content[0].text);
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

Users searching for "Mumbai engineering contacts" got back all Mumbai contacts regardless of department. Manoj logged the vector search results and their similarity scores — Mumbai contacts from marketing had high scores because "Mumbai" dominated the embedding similarity, drowning out the "engineering" signal. His fix: extract structured filters (city, department) from the query using Claude Haiku, then apply them as MongoDB pre-filters before vector ranking. This hybrid approach ensures exact-match fields like city and department are filtered precisely, while vector search handles fuzzy name/role matching. Search precision jumped from 40% to 95%.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Vector search conflates multiple filter criteriaExtract structured filters and apply as pre-filters
Filter extraction fails on ambiguous queriesFall back to pure vector search if no filters extracted
Over-filtering returns zero resultsProgressively relax filters: department → city → no filter

How to Validate

  • Search "Mumbai engineering" — verify all results have department=engineering
  • Search ambiguous query — verify graceful fallback to vector-only
  • Measure precision and recall on test query set
  • Verify filter extraction handles variations ("eng", "engineering", "engg")
